source.title: JUCAL: Jointly Calibrating Aleatoric and Epistemic Uncertainty in Classification Tasks

confidence_rationale: JUCAL demonstrates that jointly calibrating aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty requires a separate calibration procedure that standard temperature scaling cannot provide -- the two uncertainties have different structural sources that must be treated independently. This weakens Q-013's hybridize outcome by showing that a deterministic JEPA backbone cannot simply derive well-calibrated epistemic uncertainty from dispersion alone: explicit joint calibration is needed, and that calibration requires the two channels to be structurally distinct.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Classical temperature scaling fails to balance aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty in classifier ensembles; JUCAL jointly calibrates both by optimising two separate scaling constants, outperforming temperature scaling by up to 15-20% on NLL and prediction set size.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-013 asks whether JEPA's deterministic representation plus a derived dispersion measure can match explicit stochastic uncertainty heads for REE precision routing. JUCAL's finding that even in ensembles -- which have a natural source of both uncertainty types -- joint calibration requires treating the two as structurally distinct signals suggests that collapsing both into a single dispersion term would lose the calibration needed for reliable precision routing in E3.
mapping.mapping_caveat: JUCAL studies classification ensembles, not JEPA-style joint-embedding architectures or dynamics world models. The calibration challenge it documents (imbalanced aleatoric/epistemic weighting) is a general problem in uncertainty estimation but has not been demonstrated specifically in JEPA latent spaces. The paper does not address REE-style precision routing or sequential decision-making -- transfer to the REE context requires several additional assumptions.
mapping.source_context: Classifier ensembles on classification benchmarks, static inputs, no temporal or sequential structure.

confidence_components.notes: arXiv preprint, not yet peer-reviewed at time of entry. The core finding (joint calibration of two structurally distinct uncertainty types outperforms unified temperature scaling) is well-motivated and the result is plausible, but the preprint has not been independently replicated. Transfer to JEPA/REE context requires multiple analogical steps.
